name: Alto Alentejo Portuguese
notes: Alentejano Portuguese (Portalegre, Évora, Beja; Alto Alentejo type). Modelled features: (1) DELETION of final unstressed high vowels -u/-i/-e — Cintra (1971) ‘outro dos fenómenos mais típicos desta região’; encoded as allophone rule ALE_FINAL_HIGH_VOWEL_DELETION (word-final, unstressed; final /ɐ/ from -a is spared, per the source). (2) INTERVOCALIC /d/ deletion (nada→[ˈnaɐ], vida→[ˈviɐ]); positional_graphemes d/intervocalic→[]. (3) /ej/ → [e] and /ow/ → [o] monophthongs (leite→[ˈletɨ], primeiro→[pɼiˈmeɼu]). (4) meu-class /ew/ → [e] (meu→[me]) via the graphemes overlay. (5) Southern vocalism and vowel breaking [ɛ͜ɐ] in some lexical environments (allophones ɛ). (6) Dark coda /l/→[ł] and coda sibilants inherited from the pt-PT base allophone_rules. Both varieties are central-southern and therefore already share the standard’s unstressed reduction (inherited from pt-PT). DIVERGENCE FROM GOLD: the ep_dialects expert set (n=30) transcribes final vowels in full and does not encode Cintra’s deletion, so ALE_FINAL_HIGH_VOWEL_DELETION lowers agreement with that small-n gold while being the cited, linguistically-correct realisation — documented, not tuned away.

ancestors.notes: Standard European Portuguese; Alentejo diverges mainly in open vowel quality and transitional seseo-adjacent features near the Spanish border

sources.author: Cintra, L. F. Lindley
sources.title: Nova proposta de classificação dos dialectos galego-portugueses
sources.publisher: Boletim de Filologia (Centro de Estudos Filológicos, Lisboa), 22: 81–116
sources.notes: Foundational classification of European Portuguese dialects. Delimits the Barlavento Algarvio by the isogloss of stressed-/u/ palatalisation (‘a palatalização, em maior ou menor grau, da vogal tónica u’) and lists final unstressed high-vowel deletion (‘a queda das vogais finais -u, -i (ou -e), outro dos fenómenos mais típicos desta região’) as a central-southern (Alto Alentejo / Beira Baixa) feature.

sources.author: Brissos, Fernando
sources.title: New insights into Portuguese central-southern dialects: understanding their present and past forms through acoustic data from stressed vowels
sources.notes: Acoustic study of central-southern EP vowel systems. Documents Algarvio stressed /u/ → [y] ([tˈyd] ‘tudo’, [lˈymɨ] ‘lume’) and final unstressed [u] deletion/reduction ([sˈœʃt] ‘cesto’); cites Lüdtke (1957) and Maia (1975–78) that the /u/ palatalisation is stressed-only.

sources.author: Boléo, M.P.
sources.title: Estudos de linguística portuguesa e românica

stress.notes: Acordo Ortográfico 1990 conventions: paroxytone default; written accents override; final -i/-u/-r/-l/-z/-x/-im/-om/-um/-ns and nasal diphthongs attract final stress. Unmarked -em/-am stay paroxytone (homem, falam).

allophone_rules.notes: Deletion of final unstressed high vowels — Cintra (1971): ‘a queda das vogais finais -u, -i (ou -e), outro dos fenómenos mais típicos desta região’ (Alto Alentejo / Beira Baixa). In pt-PT the final unstressed vowels surface as [u] (from graphic o/u) and [ɨ] (from e); this rule drops them word-finally (gosto→[ˈɡɔʃt], noite→[ˈnojt]). Brissos (2014): ‘Final unstressed [u] disappears or is reduced to [ɨ]’. Conditioned word_final + unstressed so it never touches stressed or word-medial vowels; final /ɐ/ (from -a) is deliberately excluded, matching the source, which lists only final -u, -i, -e. This is a categorical realisation of a variable process; see notes on the divergence from the small-n ep_dialects gold.
